speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape five. I'm conducting an interview, with Mr. Mojsze Ganc, in Melbourne, Australia. You were saying who you were employed by, in Melbourne, as a furrier. What did you do after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of job did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked in a place. I worked in a factory, little factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you-- when did-- how many--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working till 1958. From 1948 till 1958, I was working at David's Brother for the fur, the fur, the fur trade. In 1958, the time, this was a-- was a little-- a little bit like a crisis. It was little bit depression. And the boss start to sack people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I bought a milk bar. In 1958, I bought a milk bar. In North Melbourne, we bought a milk bar. And we were-- I, I was in  the milk bar until 1968. How many children did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three, three children already.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sam and Pesa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were the three children by the time you were living in the milk bar?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were students. Sam was a student in high school. That's got to be 14, 15, something like that-- 16. And Pesa was maybe about five years old   or something like that, six years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe, to me, the-- where you lived and how your family survived in that environment?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the milk bar, where you worked?

speaker: interviewee
text: The milk bar was very-- very bad conditions, because the accommodation was very small, very small place to live. And we were living very compact-- bad conditions. But we survived. We survived till 1968.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then your, your oldest-- your older son, was he studying by then?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older  son already nearly finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did he manage to study in such a small area?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have a little bath. We took out the bath from the-- from this little room, and we put the bath in a shed. And from this room, we make a room for him, so he can study, so he can finish his, his, his, his pharmacy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What effect have your experiences had on you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your war experiences? What experiences have your war experiences had on you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I'm telling you the truth. Between you and me, like we say, I am very happy that I went through the Red Army. That meant I did something. I did something to kill a few-- a few Germans. That's what I'm proud. And more proud, I am-- I'm not going to lie-- I'm still alive. I didn't get killed. But that's why I was very proud that I was in Red Army and I did something to kill somebody. Maybe, I, myself, I didn't-- I  don't know. When you are shooting, you  don't know you kill somebody. But the ammunition, what we are deliver to the front-- I was, a, a, a short time-- was in a, in a, in a group for to deliver the ammunition to the, to the, to the front. The ammunition that we delivered to the front, they, they, for sure-- they did something. That  killed somebody. That's what I-- I'm proud of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you an observant Jew in, in Melbourne, now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not especially.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you belong to a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I go to pray, but I'm not a member, not a special member.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you observe high holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why have you chosen to tell your story now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? Because let the people know. If-- if my story will come to the public, into the open, let the people know how people was living. Not only in, in-- under the German occupation, in, in Russia, how people was living, how people was, was, was suffering and not living better. That's what I wanted to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for sharing your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, I want to tell you, when I got your phone to be on interview, and I was reading the questions, what I would-- what they ask, if I was in the ghettos, in camps, hiding, in partisan resistance, I thought to myself, I was not there. I was not in the resistance. I was not in-- matter of fact, I never saw a SS or a Gestapo man in uniform. I only saw it when we was-- when I was in the army, when we got them as prisoners of war. That's when I saw them. And I thought, maybe Barbara will say, no, I'm not suitable to be interviewed. But I am happy they accepted me, interview. A matter of fact, the [INAUDIBLE] story, I had written to the magazine about this one. And, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: And another thing what I want to say, our slogan is the Shoah slogan. The survivors' slogan is not to forget and not to forgive. We say only-- we mean only the bloody Germans. And what about the other nations what they helped to kill the Yiddishe people? I say, if the other nations wouldn't be-- wouldn't help the Germans to kill the Yiddishe people, 6 million people wouldn't be killed. It is a  saying in Yiddish, some people they are [NON-ENGLISH]. Do you know  what I mean? Some people, they are more religious than the pope is.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some nations was more interested to kill Yiddishe people than-- the Germans didn't come in  mind to do something like that. They was going and looking every secret places, and they'd find the     Jewish people. They'd pull them out, and they'd give them to the Germans. If this wouldn't have happened, if the, the big nations wouldn't do like that, for  sure, 6 million people wouldn't be killed, but much-- much less. That's why I say, we shouldn't forget only the Polish people, Ukrainian people, Latvia, [? Litwa's ?] people. They helped. They helped. And they should be punished much worser for the Germans. Us, we know it's a  law, an international law, if someone help a murder or a murderer or a thief-- if the man, which he steal the goods, if he wouldn't have where to sell the goods, he wouldn't steal. If the Germans wouldn't have the Polish people, the Latvia people, the other people wouldn't help, wouldn't have to help them, they wouldn't kill that many people. They wouldn't be able to do it. That is my opinion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mojsze, how old are you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you still working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two, two, a few hours in two days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you do with your time in Melbourne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I write something. I read something. That's all. Not, not, not special. Not special.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for sharing your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait  a minute, I will tell you. Because we have time. Still, I've got time. When I was in hospital, when I was shot and went to hospital, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and that time was November-- December, month, 1944. December month, 1944. At that time, the-- the United Nations started to be built, to, to be-- establishes the United Nations. We used to get, every day, the paper, [NON-ENGLISH]. And every day in the papers was big articles about the laws, the regulations for the United-- the future United Nations will have-- will have as the duty. I was reading. I could-- I, I, I read Russian, but some-- this is not very, very, very properly understand everything. But next to me was an Uzbek, a high school teacher, an educated man. Take him, we both was reading the articles. And we-- and give me  to understand them. Then, after 10 or 12 days, the old article is finished-- published in the paper. I told him-- look, he was so happy. No more war, finished. No more war. Dear me, me,  me, no more war, there will be. I tell him. My name is Mojsze Ganc. I'm a Polish Jew. As I tell everywhere I, I was, I am a Polish Jew. I was not afraid. You will see. I don't know what will be with me. You don't know what will be with you, if you will be alive. If you will be alive in the future, you will see the United Nations will be like the League of Nations. They'll not  matter a cracker. All right. In 1985, I'm already in Australia. I'm reading the papers that in 1985, 40 years United Nations-- because the United Nations was established in '45. '85 was 40 years-- United Nations celebrating 40 years  of  the United Nations. Kings, queens, presidents will go to the United Nations. They're celebrating. One night, middle  of the night, I wake up. And I was-- remind me what I was reading in the paper, the, the celebration that I got celebrating, the 40th celebration of the United Nations. And it reminded me of the story what I was have a talk with the Uzbek in the hospital, when I tell him the United Nations will not matter a cracker. 2 o'clock in the morning, I got out of bed. I sit there and write a letter to the secretary of the United Nations-- not good English. As you see, I'm speaking like that, not good English. And I didn't let-- and I wrote to the United Nations general secretary like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please tell me what you've done in the-- during the 40 years the United Nations exists? Did they stop one war? Since 1945, the war finished, was a-- till 1985 was already a few wars, Korea, in Vietnam, in Israel, Iraq-Iran. And there were  other little wars. Did you stopped? You make a peace between the nations? Can you tell me one point? And I've written to him again. How can it happen, Arafat, a murderer, to walk in the United Nations chamber, to deliver his poisonous speech, with his gun in front of him like a butcher with his knife? How can it be allowed to do like that, a cheek to go in,  in the United Nations? And nobody was saying nothing. And I write all that. Also  I mention, how it can happen that only one democratic country, Israel, exists in the Middle East to be called racist? How it can happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hadn't got the address from the United Nations, but I just written on the envelope, United Nations, America, USA. In two weeks' time,  I got a reply. The reply doesn't suit me. Doesn't-- it's not the, the reply that should be. But the matter of fact, I got a reply. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When Arafat got his peace-- peace prize, Nobel Peace Prize. At the same time, when Arafat got the peace prize, 12 people got killed at the same  time. something-- 12 Yiddishe people were killed. I'd written a letter to the president from the-- from the-- from the peace-- Nobel Prize. I written to him. How can you give a Nobel Peace Prize for a man which is not peace. He's still killing people. I got a reply for it. They wrote a reply. This is a matter of fact, I'd written I'd written the editor from

speaker: interviewee
text: I read that the, the, the in Germany-- we should be happy-- in Germany, they're teaching in the school-- in school, the children about the Holocaust. And they are all right. They are teaching. They're learning. At that time, about two years ago, you remember, it was the skinheads was demonstrating. And the Greek people was killed in Australia, you remember? And I'd written a letter to And Age published my letter. How you, you can see you were so happy, that the Germans, they are already educated about the Holocaust. What about that? The, the, the, the, the, the Nazi party is growing. A few days later, I got a letter from the-- from a-- from a antisemite, this letter. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What do you think about that? Anyway. I thank you. I thank you, Mojsze.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you have some more questions, here-- I was [INAUDIBLE] and  I was preparing myself, much more, much more, but already-- I am already a little bit exhausted, tired. You understand? You understand?

speaker: interviewer
text: I do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I tell you, and I think to say-- I, I, I, I, I give you much information, which is usable for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I really want to thank you sincerely, Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph is my grandmother, my grandfather, my mother's parents. I am there, my brother, which he died, like, not long ago, in Russia, my brother, which he was killed, my mother, my younger brother, and my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you. Mojsze, can you please describe the people in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- that is people-- that's, that's people-- older people, not my age, older people. And the man from the-- the second from the right, is a poet. He died recently. A few weeks ago, he died in Israel. He was 90 years old, a poet. He was coming to home to visit his relatives in France. And I was sitting in the park, in the garden at [INAUDIBLE]. And I was sitting and discussing and discussing. You can see that the poet, he's holding a book of Juvenal in his hand. And they saw me. They knew me, because I was-- they was-- they was from the same organization, the older people. And I was from the younger people. They saw me there. They called me over. They said, Mojsze, come on. We have a photo we're taking with you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you describe this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a group from our youth organization. The man from [NON-ENGLISH] a, a, a leader from the left-wing Palestinian Party. We are the youth organization. One man,  from the middle, was sitting on the middle, he was shifting from [INAUDIBLE] to another side, to another turn. And we're making the photo when he was left with [INAUDIBLE] photo, a special photograph. That's-- I was there. This  is me and my former girlfriend, my wife later. We are already here in this photo.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken-- [INAUDIBLE] The date  is mentioned. I think it's 1935. It's mentioned on the other side from the photo.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Can you tell me about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photograph is the place where I was working as a furrier. In the middle-- in the middle is the boss. And the next is the worker. And I am-- I am in the-- in the left-hand, from the boss. I am in the left-hand.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see on the wall hangs a diploma? That means he is-- my boss is a-- is a-- is a specialist meister, a first grade meister. And next to that is a-- is a, a, a, a, a comment. You have to buy-- and it's taxation-- a patent, that you have a right to be on-- to carry on with a, a, a, a business. Thank you. Can you tell me about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph is done after we got married. In 1940, in Vitebsk, in White Russia, we were married. We was a happy couple getting married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me about the people in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph is done after my little daughter was born, in 1955, something like that. That is-- the left-- the right side is Jack and Sam and me, with my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph is done by some occasion. I can't remember what kind of occasion it is about. It is a wedding or something. I simple-- I can't remember what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Can you tell me, Mojsze, about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: My photograph. When I was reading in the paper that Elie Wiesel got a, a Nobel Prize for Peace, Peace Nobel Prize, I-- and I-- and I-- and I read in the paper that he got so many congratulations and got wishes from big, big, big personalities, I thought to myself, I will also send a congratulations to the-- to the-- to Mr. Wiesel. I haven't got his address. I written on the envelope, Mr. Wiesel. He receives just-- very recently, he received a Nobel Prize for Peace. After a few weeks, the envelope was coming back, torn already, squashed. And I don't know what to do. I want to send a card. And I don't-- the photo-- and I-- the, the, the-- I want to send a congratulations to him, and I can't. I haven't got his address. I asked the secretary, my son's secretary, from the-- from the office. And she ringed the American embassy. And she got-- from their embassy, she got their address. And I sent him the letter, the congratulations. He sent me back a letter. He has written to me, if I will be in Melbourne, sometimes, we'll meet each other. And when he was in Melbourne, in 1986, he keeped his word. And I met him in Melbourne Town Hall. And I spoke with him. He asked to make a photo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me about who is in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph is a question like that. When my grandchildrens' speech night was one night, in Melbourne Concert Hall, when the curtains was coming up, and I look on the stage. And I saw, on this stage, is not the Israel and the Australian flag.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought  to myself, how can such an occasion not be the Israeli and Australian flag? I written to the-- to the headmaster from Mount Scopus. He did reply to me, that was a mistake. That was a-- but when the other grandchild was there on  the occasion of another speech night, again it was not the, the the flags on, on, on, on on the stage.

speaker: interviewee
text: I written again. And they tell me they will. Next time, I was all ready, but it when my son-- grandson, David, was graduated. And I went there. And the flags was already on the stage. They was already on the stage. And I saw the headmaster. And he said to me, because you are the man, which makes the flags to be on the stage on such an occasion, they called me over to the ambassador. They tell the ambassador the story. And he tell  them, call a photograph man. Let's make a picture with  me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me about this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: This document is the Yiddishe [INAUDIBLE], very, very difficult. When we got married, we have to-- this, this, this-- that's written with the-- And we can see, the first line was not written properly, because I didn't have the ink. There was run-out from ink. They didn't have ink. But I find ink, and it's done all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a marriage certificate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me who this letter was from?

speaker: interviewee
text: This letter, when   I got back from Russia to Poland, and I saw in Poland is not a good place to live, because there was pogrom and killing Yiddishe people, I decided to get in touch with my relations in Australia to get a permit. And I'd written a few letters, and I didn't get a-- I didn't get a reply. I asked somebody I met, which he can write and read English. He'd written a letter to the Victoria Police. It asked all the addresses from the people. And they sent me the addresses. And I sent it to my cousin, Harry, and he sent me-- and he sent me the-- a permit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me about this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: This document, this photo is done, in Red Army hospital, after I got shot in my left arm, by a German sniper. And what year? That was 1944. I was in hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: My arm was in, in, in, in, in plaster.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me about this booklet?

speaker: interviewee
text: This booklet is the membership from the trade union, from the factory I was working. 15,000 people was working at this factory, clothing factory. I was working there. And this was my trade union-- trade union book.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vitebsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1939, when we arrived, to-- in the town Vitebsk, I went to the, the, the the state government. And they gave me a recommendation to try-- to get work in a big factory, clothing factory. They-- from-- the factory's name is [NON-ENGLISH]. I was working in this factory until 1941, till the war break out, and  I escape from Vitebsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you describe this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: This document was after I had written to my cousin, in Melbourne, and he got a, a permit for me and my wife and my younger-- my young son, Jack, to come to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you explain this document, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This document? Mr. Wiesel, when he got his Nobel Peace Prize, people sent him-- used to send him congratulations, good wishes. And I thought to myself, why I shouldn't send him my congratulations? I sent him a letter and congratulated him. And he replied to me, when he will be back in-- when he will be sometimes in Melbourne, he will meet me, he will see me. And he will be able to talk with me. When he was in Melbourne, in 1986, I met him in Town Hall. And we had a talk with him. And a picture was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. Yevtushenko was here in-- In 1994, he was here for two-- for, for-- and he delivered his speeches. And we was reading this paper about him. I had written a letter to him, and I-- my opinion about these poems. And he replied to me, the letter. And he sent me a gift, this book, his last published book, in, in-- poems, in English, in Russian. He sent me this for a-- for a present.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Can you tell me about this letter, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I read in Age, a, a-- the editorial from sometimes-- What date is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: What date is this?

speaker: interviewer
text: August '93.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes in August '93, I was reading in an-- the editorial that, that we should be happy the German government, the German schools, the children, they are learning about the Holocaust. But at that time, it was very, very much anti, anti-Nazi, Nazi demonstrations. They was killing a few Greek people. And they was killing--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had written to how I can be happy with the, the-- they, they are Nazis. They are teaching. We killed them-- if they are all already so many, many demonstrations and then killing? And they-- [INAUDIBLE] the Nazis. And a few days later, I got this letter from him. What-- this, this one-- this one will not be one that they kill.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about this letter, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: In   June, 1955, I went to the city by the tram. And I noticed, on the Arts Centre, was-- a swastika was painted on the [? fifth ?] floor. I went in the Arts Centre, and I was going to ask somebody, but nobody was there. A man-- I asked a man if I can speak with the president or something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: They  tell me the manager is not here. He will come back after, after lunch. And I had written a letter to the, to the, to the manager of the Arts Centre. And I, and I, and I tell him, did you know what that swastika is?

speaker: interviewee
text: And how you suppose to go to-- somebody can go up on the fifth floor in the Arts Centre to paint a swastika. He was painting this swatiska not like a third grade painter. A first grade painter, it was. He done the job properly. And I sent a letter to him. And he sent  me back a reply. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. --your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, to my left, my older son, Jack, my right, my younger son, Sam, my daughter-in-law, Lydia, my daughter-in-law Olivia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pesa. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: He forgot who she  was.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is her cousin-- and Erin. Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's all right. We got a nice [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Would someone like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll say some words. I'd like to take the opportunity of thanking Spielberg and the Shoah Foundation for undertaking the enormity of this venture that he has. For us, personally, as a family, and for my father, in particular, it's provided him a great purging opportunity. And I think that he's probably told the interviewer and the tape much more than he's taught us as a family. And we, as a family, are obviously most interested to view and listen and watch and learn from his experiences. The Holocaust has obviously been horrific. And there are many stories, I'm sure, that are on file. Our story, or my father's story, probably pales into insignificance with respect to some of the enormity of others. But what it has done is it has given him and our family enormous strength of character and the ability to focus on success and focus on well-being and family. And therefore, in spite of all the horrible things that the Holocaust has provided and forced upon the Jewish nation, I think one of the benefits is it has, has strengthened us. And hopefully, we'll gain from the strength that that has given us. And we three children of Mojsze-- and of the-- and the, I think it's nine grandchildren, and our ongoing generations will benefit from the strength that we've gained from our father's experiences, as horrific as they may be. We also-- maybe I can take the opportunity of thanking our father and my mother for not burdening us with the guilt of the Holocaust and allowing us to, in our own way, become strong and successful individuals. But probably importantly enough is that we won't and will never forget the evil and the horrific events that the Holocaust has caused our faith.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess I  just want to say, it's pretty difficult to imagine how you can make a decision, that my father had to make back then, and how one would make a decision under those circumstances. We don't face anything like it today. I mean we live in total isolation from the sort of problems that existed. It's really hard to imagine what sort of experiences you could have gone through. I guess by what you're documenting here, we'll be able to, in some small way, perhaps relieve some of these experiences that we could just never imagine other than through exercises such as this. And  I hope we'll never have to go through that experience ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pesa? OK, thank you. Thank you, all.
